critique.summary: The article explores the technical optimizations behind the 1999 game RollerCoaster Tycoon, highlighting its development almost entirely in Assembly language by Chris Sawyer. It discusses specific programming techniques and design decisions that enabled the game to run efficiently on late 1990s hardware, such as fine-grained data types, bit-shifting for math operations, and gameplay design choices that reduce computational load.
mainPoints: RollerCoaster Tycoon was primarily written in Assembly language, which contributed to its high performance on 1999 hardware.
The game uses fine-grained data types for money values to optimize memory and processing.
Bit-shifting operations replace some multiplication and division to improve speed.
Game design choices, such as guests wandering randomly instead of pathfinding to destinations, reduce expensive computations.
Pathfinding is limited in scope and selectively applied to avoid performance spikes.
Guests do not collide or avoid each other, allowing many to occupy the same space without costly calculations, though overcrowding affects guest happiness.
OpenRCT2, a fan-made reimplementation, provides insight into the original game's code and optimizations.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely accurate and consistent with known information about RollerCoaster Tycoon and its development. It is well-documented that Chris Sawyer wrote much of the game in Assembly, which was unusual for the time but contributed to its performance. The described optimizations, such as bit-shifting and selective pathfinding, are plausible and supported by the OpenRCT2 project's reverse engineering efforts. The article correctly notes that modern compilers reduce the performance gap between Assembly and high-level languages. The discussion about game design influencing performance is also credible and aligns with common practices in game development. However, the article is based on secondary sources and reverse engineering rather than direct original source code, which limits definitive verification.
considerations: The article relies heavily on the OpenRCT2 reverse engineering project rather than the original source code, which has not been publicly released.
It does not provide direct quotes or citations from Chris Sawyer or official development documentation.
The article focuses on technical and design optimizations but does not discuss other factors such as hardware constraints or marketing influences.
It assumes readers have some familiarity with programming concepts like Assembly and bit-shifting, which may limit accessibility.
The article does not compare RollerCoaster Tycoon’s optimization techniques with those of other contemporary games in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on low-level optimization techniques in game development, especially assembly and bitwise operations, with some debate over compiler optimizations and the role of human insight in performance tuning. Several commenters share historical context and technical clarifications, while others recommend related resources or express enthusiasm for the topic.

comments.author: netcoyote
comments.text:
Warcraft 1 (1994), Warcraft 2 (1995), and StarCraft (1998) all use power-of-2 aligned map sizes (64 blocks, 128 blocks, and 256 blocks) so the shift-factor could be pre-computed to avoid division/multiplication, which was dang slow on those old 386/486 computers.

Each map block was 2x2 cells, and each cell, 8x8 pixels. Made rendering background cells and fog-of-war overlays very straightforward assembly language.

All of Warcraft/etc. had only a few thousand lines of assembly language to render maps/sprites/fonts/fog-of-war into the offscreen buffer, and to blit from the offscreen buffer to the screen.

The rest of the code didn't need to be in assembly, which is too time-consuming to write for code where the performance doesn't matter. Everything else was written in portable assembler, by which I mean C.

Edit:

By way of comparison, Blackthorne for Super Nintendo was all 85816 assembly. The Genesis version (Motorola 68000) and DOS version (Intel 80386) were manually transcribed into their respective assembly languages.

The PC version of Blackthorne also had a lot of custom assembler macros to generate 100K of rendering code to do pixel-scrollable chunky-planar VGA mode X (written by Bryan Waters - https://www.mobygames.com/person/5641/bryan-waters/).

At Blizzard we learned from working on those console app ports that writing assembly code takes too much programmer time.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed historical and technical context about assembly use in classic games, accurately describing optimization practices and trade-offs.

comments.author: applfanboysbgon
comments.text:
> Imagine a programmer asking a game designer if they could change their formula to use an 8 instead of a 9.5 because it is a number that the CPU prefers to calculate with. There is a very good argument to be made that a game designer should never have to worry about the runtime performance characteristics of binary arithmetic in their life, that’s a fate reserved for programmers

Numeric characteristics are absolutely still a consideration for game designers even in 2026, one that influences what numbers they use in their game designs. The good ones, anyways. There are, of course, also countless bad developers/designers who ignore these things these days, but not because it is free to do so; rather, because they don't know better, and in many cases it is one of many silent contributing factors to a noticeable decrease in the quality of their game.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully argues that numeric optimization remains relevant for game designers, highlighting the ongoing importance of performance awareness.

comments.author: troad
comments.text:
> When reading through OpenRCT2’s source, there is a common syntax that you rarely see in modern code, lines like this:

> NewValue = OldValue << 2;

I gently disagree with the framing of this section. Bit shifts are used all the time in low level code. They're not just some archaic optimisation, they're also a natural way of working with binary data (aka all data on a computer). Modern low level code continues to use lots of bit shifts, bitwise operators, etc.

I understand that the author is using 'modern code' to mean 'higher level code', but in so doing they miss out on an interesting observation about one of the most essential enduring differences between low-level and high-level programming, which seems relevant to an article about optimisation.

Low-level programming is absolutely crucial to performant games. Even if you're not doing low-level programming yourself, you're almost certainly using an engine or library that does.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly clarifies that bitwise operations remain common and essential in low-level programming, countering the article's implication that they are archaic.

comments.author: fweimer
comments.text: What language is this article talking where compilers don't optimize multiplication and division by powers of two? Even for division of signed integers, current compilers emit inline code that handles positive and negative values separately, still avoiding the division instruction (unless when optimizing for size, of course).
comments.analysis: The comment accurately points out that modern compilers do optimize division and multiplication by powers of two, contradicting the article's claim.

comments.author: youarentrightjr
comments.analysis: This comment directly refutes a claim in the article about compilers not performing certain optimizations, providing a technically accurate correction.

comments.author: lefty2
comments.text:
> The same trick can also be used for the other direction to save a division:

> NewValue = OldValue >> 3;

You need to be careful, because this doesn't work if the value is negative. A
comments.analysis: This comment adds an important caveat about the limitations of using bit shifts as division for negative values, showing nuanced understanding.

comments.author: londons_explore
comments.text:
> it turns an optimization done out of technical necessity into a gameplay feature

And this folks is why an optimizing compiler can never beat sufficient quantities of human optimization.

The human can decide when the abstraction layers should be deliberately broken for performance reasons. A compiler cannot do that.
comments.analysis: The comment emphasizes the value of human judgment in optimization beyond what compilers can achieve, a reasonable perspective though somewhat subjective.

comments.author: HelloUsername
comments.text:
Fun read, thx! I'd also recommend more about RCT:

comments.author: sroerick
comments.text:
I had always heard about how RCT was built in Assembly, and thought it was very impressive.

The more I actually started digging into assembly, the more this task seems monumental and impossible.

I didn't know there was a fork and I'm excited to look into it
comments.analysis: This personal reflection expresses genuine interest and acknowledges the complexity of assembly programming, adding a human perspective.

comments.author: bluelightning2k
comments.text:
Great write up. Thank you. Really great!

I was reminded of the factorio blog. That game's such a huge optimization challenge even by today's standards and I believe works with the design.

One interesting thing I remember is if you have a long conveyor belt of 10,000 copper coils, you can basically simplify it to just be only the entry and exit tile are actually active. All the others don't actually have to move because nothing changes... As long as the belts are fully or uniformly saturated. So you avoid mechanics which would stop that.
comments.analysis: This comment draws a relevant analogy to another game’s optimization strategies, enriching the conversation with a practical example.

comments.author: sghiassy
comments.text:
Another great optimization is storing the year as two digits, because you only need the back half…

… oh wait, nvm. Don’t preoptimize!
comments.analysis: This comment humorously warns against premature optimization, a common and valid principle in software development.

comments.author: MisterTea
comments.text:
While it has been a while since playing RCT, one thing that was really nice about the game is that it runs flawlessly under Wine.

I really wish I could see the source code.
comments.analysis: This comment is more of a personal anecdote unrelated to the technical discussion, adding little analytical value.

comments.author: ZebusJesus
comments.text: so I don't thin the rust people are gonna be happy with non memory safe assembly
comments.analysis: This comment is vague and speculative without clear relevance or evidence, reducing its usefulness in the discussion.
